title: From Solvers to Research: Large Language Model-Driven Formal Mathematics at the Research Frontier
authors: Eric Jiang, Xiao Liang, Yikai Zhang, Yingjia Wan, Mengting Li, Haikang Deng
abstract: Recent developments in AI for Mathematics (AI4Math), especially Large Language Model (LLM)-driven theorem provers, has achieved remarkable success in formal proof generation for well-defined mathematical problems through Interactive Theorem Proving (ITP) languages. However, current systems remain fundamentally limited in tackling frontier research mathematics, such as discovering new theorems or resolving open conjectures, which are often open-ended, under-specified, and involve multiple layers of abstraction. We argue that the next leap in AI4Math systems requires a decisive shift from predefined problem-solvers to research agents that can address frontier mathematical challenges with rigorous formal mathematical reasoning. In this position paper, we provide a systematic review of the field, covering datasets, auto-formalization, and proof synthesis. More importantly, we identify core limitations of existing systems in serving as mathematical research agents, examining issues across datasets, relational structure, mathematical exploration, tool ecosystem, and human-AI collaboration, outlining a strategic road-map for the future of AI4Math.

infographic.key_insight: The paper argues that LLM-based theorem provers must evolve from solving predefined formal problems to acting as research agents capable of discovering and rigorously formalizing frontier mathematics.
infographic.engineering_relevance: For computational science and engineering, this reframes AI4Math as an infrastructure challenge: building systems that integrate formal reasoning, exploration, tools, and human collaboration for open-ended research tasks.
method: Systematically reviews the AI4Math pipeline: datasets, auto-formalization, and proof synthesis.
Identifies gaps across relational structure, mathematical exploration, tool ecosystem, and human-AI collaboration.
Outlines a research roadmap toward research-grade formal mathematical agents.
infographic.evidence_signal: The paper is a position/review article rather than an empirical benchmark study, so its support comes from a structured survey of current system capabilities and limitations described in the abstract.
infographic.watch_item: A key implementation concern is that current theorem-proving systems are still optimized for well-specified tasks, making open-ended research settings difficult to evaluate and reliably formalize.
infographic.paper_title: From Solvers to Research: Large Language Model-Driven Formal Mathematics at the Research Frontier

title: Danus: Orchestrating Mathematical Reasoning Agents with Fact-Graph Memory
authors: Jihao Liu, Guoxiong Gao, Zeming Sun, Bin Wu, Shurui Liu, Jiedong Jiang
abstract: Recent LLM-based mathematical reasoning agents have begun to tackle research-level problems and, in several cases, have contributed to the resolution of open problems. However, scaling and orchestrating such agents effectively remains challenging, due to the difficulty of coordinating parallel proof search while keeping intermediate claims organized and reliable. In this paper, we propose Danus, an orchestration system for research-level mathematical reasoning centered on a shared fact graph as a global memory-management mechanism. Danus consists of a main agent that performs planning and coordination, multiple worker agents that carry out proof search in parallel, and a stateless verifier that checks proposed mathematical claims before they are admitted into the fact graph. Each verified fact is stored together with its proof and logical dependencies, allowing the system to build long arguments incrementally while keeping the shared proof state organized. The main agent periodically summarizes the evolving proof state, redirects workers across promising directions, and supports interaction with human mathematicians through progress reports. We evaluate Danus through six research-level case studies in algebraic geometry, singularity theory, and combinatorics, illustrating how the fact-graph memory mechanism enables Danus to construct long, detailed mathematical proofs. Our results suggest that fact-graph-based orchestration provides an effective route toward scaling mathematical reasoning agents for long-horizon research problems. Danus is open source at https://github.com/frenzymath/Danus.

infographic.key_insight: Danus scales research-level mathematical reasoning by coordinating multiple agents around a shared fact graph that stores verified claims, proofs, and dependencies as global memory.
infographic.engineering_relevance: This architecture offers a practical blueprint for long-horizon proof search systems that need parallel exploration, reliable state tracking, and human-in-the-loop oversight.
method: Main agent plans and redirects search across worker agents.
Workers generate proof attempts in parallel.
A stateless verifier admits only checked facts into the shared fact graph.
infographic.evidence_signal: The abstract reports six research-level case studies spanning algebraic geometry, singularity theory, and combinatorics to illustrate long proof construction.
infographic.watch_item: Performance may depend on verifier quality and on maintaining a consistent fact graph as proofs become very large and interdependent.
infographic.paper_title: Danus: Orchestrating Mathematical Reasoning Agents with Fact-Graph Memory

title: Evaluating SageMath-Augmented LLM Agents for Computational and Experimental Mathematics
abstract: Recent advances in AI for Mathematics have focused largely on autoformalization and theorem proving, leaving the role of Computer Algebra Systems (CAS) in agentic LLM workflows underexplored. We propose a ReAct-style agentic setup that combines LLM reasoning with verifiable feedback from SageMath, together with Context7 for the up-to-date documentation. We evaluate this agentic setup across frontier models for solving research-level mathematical problems from the RealMath benchmark in a setting that emulates a computational-mathematics research loop. We also propose a refinement to the RealMath benchmark by introducing a multi-step post-processing procedure and a multi-stage validation pipeline, both of which improve the quality and reliability of the extracted problem set. Our experiments reveal substantial performance gains from SageMath access across all evaluated models on +9.7~pp on average, the gains range from 1.5~pp to 27.8~pp and narrow the gap between open-weight and closed models. Qwen~3.7-Max benefits from SageMath the most, while GPT-5.5 achieves the highest solve rate of $75.2\%$ and the lowest token usage among tool-enabled configurations. Our findings suggest that CAS-augmented agents represent a promising direction for assisting mathematicians in computational exploration, and we believe that this work is a step towards automated conjecture discovery. The project repository is available online.

infographic.key_insight: Adding SageMath to an LLM agent significantly improves research-level math problem solving by providing verifiable computational feedback during reasoning.
infographic.engineering_relevance: This shows that CAS-augmented agent workflows can boost accuracy and efficiency for computational mathematics tasks, especially when paired with current documentation access.
method: ReAct-style LLM agent with SageMath tool access for step-by-step computation and verification
Context7 used to retrieve up-to-date documentation during problem solving
Evaluated across frontier models on the RealMath benchmark with a refined multi-step extraction and validation pipeline
infographic.evidence_signal: Across the evaluated models, SageMath access improved performance by 9.7 percentage points on average, with gains ranging from 1.5 to 27.8 points; GPT-5.5 reached 75.2% solve rate.
infographic.watch_item: Results depend on the quality of tool integration and benchmark post-processing, so gains may not transfer directly to settings with weaker tool support or different problem distributions.
infographic.paper_title: Evaluating SageMath-Augmented LLM Agents for Computational and Experimental Mathematics

title: Physics-Informed Neural Embeddings of PDE Solution Families
authors: Raul Jimenez, Svitlana Mayboroda, Pavlos Protopapas, Leonid Sarieddine, David N. Spergel, Pedro Tarancón-Álvarez
abstract: We introduce a physics-informed framework for learning finite-dimensional embeddings of solution families of partial differential equations. The method uses a multihead Physics-Informed Neural Network in which a shared body learns a latent manifold representing the solution space, while linear heads reconstruct individual solutions associated with different initial conditions. A head-orthogonalization penalty removes degeneracies in the latent representation and stabilizes the principal-component spectrum across training realizations. Because the initial condition is built into the network output by construction, these principal components measure the additional variability the network learns on top of the initial profile, not the full solution itself. We apply the method to the one-dimensional viscous Burgers equation, with the heat and wave equations as robustness checks. For a latent dimension $n_b=20$, the learned manifolds exhibit pronounced effective dimensional reduction: for Burgers dynamics, only $2$-$4$ principal components capture about $95\%$ of the latent-space variance, while $4$-$7$ capture about $99\%$, depending on the initial-condition family; the same qualitative compression holds for the heat and wave equations. We also split the wavenumber axis into bands (``Fourier shells'') and measure how much each band contributes to every principal component. The resulting frequency profile is invariant under the change-of-basis freedom that the orthogonalization penalty leaves in the latent space, and is therefore reproducible across independent training runs. More broadly, this establishes the learned spectral profiles and principal components as robust observables of solution-manifold geometry.

infographic.key_insight: A physics-informed multihead neural network can learn a low-dimensional latent manifold for PDE solution families, revealing robust principal components and spectral profiles beyond the initial condition.
infographic.engineering_relevance: This provides a reusable way to compress, analyze, and compare large families of PDE solutions with stable geometric features that could aid reduced-order modeling and scientific discovery.
method: Shared network body learns a latent embedding of the solution manifold.
Multiple linear heads reconstruct solutions for different initial conditions.
Head-orthogonalization penalizes degeneracies and stabilizes the latent basis; Fourier-shell analysis measures frequency contributions to each principal component.
infographic.evidence_signal: On the 1D viscous Burgers equation, a latent dimension of 20 yielded only 2-4 principal components for about 95% variance and 4-7 for about 99%, with similar compression behavior on heat and wave equation checks.
infographic.watch_item: Because the initial condition is built into the output by construction, the latent components capture residual variation on top of the initial profile, so interpretation depends on the chosen architecture and basis freedom.
infographic.paper_title: Physics-Informed Neural Embeddings of PDE Solution Families

title: Learning the Energy Landscapes of Dynamical Systems via Energetic Variational Optimal Transport under Data Quantity--Quality Trade-offs
authors: Shun Wang, Chun Liu, Wenrui Hao
abstract: Dynamic optimal transport unifies optimal transport, fluid mechanics, and gradient-flow theory within a continuous dynamical framework, offering a geometry-aware language for applications across physics, biology, and machine learning. However, conventional formulations cast it as a constrained optimization problem that must explicitly satisfy the continuity equation, hindering the reconstruction of the underlying dynamics directly from data. We propose the energetic variational method for dynamic optimal transport (EVMDOT), which reformulates it within an energetic variational framework by combining the flow map, the least action principle, and the maximum dissipation principle. The flow map recasts the constrained problem as an unconstrained one by automatically enforcing the continuity equation, while the balance between the conservative and dissipative forces determines the velocity field. Applied to the Fokker--Planck equation, the EVMDOT reconstructs both the energy landscape and the Waddington landscape directly from time-series density data. Through numerical experiments, we reveal that the EVMDOT achieves an intrinsic balance between data quantity and data quality: a sufficient data quantity compensates for limited data quality, making the reconstruction robust to the choice of the observation window. We further apply the EVMDOT to the Alzheimer's Disease Neuroimaging Initiative (ADNI) dataset to infer the potential landscape of amyloid-$β$ and tau, revealing two wells corresponding to the cognitively normal and Alzheimer's disease stages and the transition pathway between them.

infographic.key_insight: EVMDOT turns dynamic optimal transport into an unconstrained energetic variational problem, letting the underlying flow and energy landscape be learned directly from time-series density data.
infographic.engineering_relevance: This provides a data-driven way to reconstruct potential landscapes and transition pathways in complex dynamical systems, with potential use in physical modeling and biomedical state-transition analysis.
method: Use the flow map to enforce the continuity equation automatically
Combine least action and maximum dissipation to recover velocity fields
Apply to Fokker--Planck dynamics to infer energy and Waddington landscapes
infographic.evidence_signal: Numerical experiments and ADNI data show the method can reconstruct landscapes robustly, with data quantity partly compensating for lower data quality.
infographic.watch_item: Landscape recovery may still depend on the chosen observation window and the quality/coverage of the sampled density trajectories.
infographic.paper_title: Learning the Energy Landscapes of Dynamical Systems via Energetic Variational Optimal Transport under Data Quantity--Quality Trade-offs

title: Trustworthy Machine Learning through the Lens of Combinatorial Optimization: Survey and Research Perspectives
abstract: Modern machine learning (ML) increasingly relies on complex models whose behavior is difficult to characterize beyond empirical performance metrics. Across a wide range of tasks, including prediction, generation, and decision-making, models with similar empirical performance can exhibit markedly different properties in terms of their transparency, interpretability, robustness, fairness, privacy, and certifiability. This survey highlights how optimization- and certification-oriented reasoning can provide a useful framework for reasoning about such differences, supporting tasks ranging from model training and selection to auditing and certification. We review and synthesize recent advances at the intersection of combinatorial optimization (CO) and trustworthy ML, covering both training and post-training tasks, including interpretable model learning, explanation generation, robustness analysis, fairness auditing, model compression, and privacy attacks and protections. Across these domains, CO formulations offer additional capabilities over purely heuristic approaches, e.g., gradient-based ones, notably global guarantees, formal certificates, and explicit treatment of trade-offs. While scalability remains an important challenge, continued progress in solvers and hybrid algorithms suggests a growing role for CO in the design and deployment of trustworthy ML systems.

infographic.key_insight: Combinatorial optimization provides a unifying framework for making machine learning systems more trustworthy by enabling formal guarantees for interpretability, robustness, fairness, privacy, and certification.
infographic.engineering_relevance: For deployed ML pipelines, CO-based methods can support model selection, auditing, and certification with explicit trade-off control beyond standard gradient-driven training.
method: Formulate trustworthiness goals as optimization or certification problems.
Use CO to generate interpretable models, explanations, and compression schemes.
Apply CO for robustness analysis, fairness auditing, and privacy attacks/protections.
infographic.evidence_signal: This is a survey synthesizing recent advances across training and post-training tasks rather than presenting a single new dataset or benchmark.
infographic.watch_item: Scalability remains a key limitation, so exact or certification-oriented CO methods may require hybrid solvers or approximations in large-scale settings.
infographic.paper_title: Trustworthy Machine Learning through the Lens of Combinatorial Optimization: Survey and Research Perspectives

title: Mathematical methods of reinforcement learning
authors: Denis Belomestny, Alexander Gasnikov, Egor Gladin, Alexey Naumov, Artemy Rubtsov, Yuri Sapronov
abstract: Reinforcement learning (RL) is increasingly grounded in tools from probability, optimization, and operator theory. This survey organizes the mathematical structures that underpin the design and analysis of modern algorithms in RL. We begin from Markov decision processes (MDPs) and the Bellman operators, emphasizing contraction mappings, monotonicity, and fixed-point theory that yield convergence guarantees and rates for value and policy iteration, and temporal-difference schemes. We then develop the optimization perspective: stochastic approximation and martingale methods, convex duality and the role of regularization linking mirror/proximal methods. Function approximation is treated through linear and non-linear settings, covering stabilization, error decomposition, and sample-complexity via concentration inequalities for dependent data and mixing processes. We further cover off-policy evaluation/learning, constrained RL and constrained MDPs (CMDPs). Throughout we unify algorithmic templates under common operator and variational lenses, highlighting both finite-sample bounds and asymptotic results. Our presentation is intended to provide a unified mathematical entry point for researchers in probability, optimization, and statistics interested in reinforcement learning.

infographic.key_insight: The paper shows that reinforcement learning can be analyzed as a unified mathematical system built from Markov decision processes, operator theory, optimization, and stochastic approximation.
infographic.engineering_relevance: This framework helps engineers reason about RL algorithm stability, convergence, sample efficiency, and constraint handling in real-world control and decision systems.
method: Analyze Bellman operators via contraction, monotonicity, and fixed-point arguments.
View RL algorithms through stochastic approximation, martingale tools, and convex duality/regularization.
Extend the framework to function approximation, off-policy learning, and constrained MDPs using concentration and variational methods.
infographic.evidence_signal: The abstract indicates a survey-style synthesis of theoretical results, including finite-sample bounds, asymptotic guarantees, and convergence rates rather than new experimental data.
infographic.watch_item: The strongest guarantees often rely on idealized assumptions such as contraction, mixing, or well-behaved approximation spaces, which may weaken in complex deep RL settings.
infographic.paper_title: Mathematical methods of reinforcement learning

title: Any-Dimensional Learning by Sampling
abstract: Many machine learning models are defined for inputs of different sizes, such as point clouds containing different numbers of points, sequences of tokens of different lengths, and graphs on different numbers of nodes. Such models are trained on finitely-many examples of necessarily limited sizes. How well do these models generalize from inputs of small size to larger inputs of size not seen during training? Furthermore, evaluating such models on large inputs is often expensive. How can we sketch large inputs to obtain smaller ones on which the model takes similar values? At the heart of both questions is the need to compare inputs of different sizes and to approximate large inputs by small ones. We present a unified approach to address these questions by using random sampling maps to compare inputs of different sizes. The sampling maps we consider are generalizations of sampling with replacement, random binning, and species sampling. We characterize the application domains in which each type of sampling is appropriate in terms of the symmetries and relations between problem instances of different sizes in the domain. Our framework yields explicit generalization and sketching rates for function classes continuous with respect to a chosen notion of sampling, encompassing large families of functions defined on sequences, graphs, and tensors of different sizes. Specific examples include moment polynomials on measures, homomorphism densities and numbers of graphs, permutation-invariant transformers, and graph neural networks.

infographic.key_insight: Random sampling maps provide a unified way to compare and compress inputs of different sizes, enabling principled generalization and sketching for variable-size data.
infographic.engineering_relevance: This gives designers of transformers, GNNs, sequence models, and graph/tensor methods a theory for extrapolating from small training inputs to larger unseen ones while reducing evaluation cost.
method: Define size-changing sampling maps to relate large and small instances.
Match sampling type to domain symmetries: replacement, binning, or species-style sampling.
Derive explicit generalization and sketching rates for sampling-continuous function classes.
infographic.evidence_signal: The abstract reports theoretical characterizations and explicit rates across families such as measures, graphs, sequences, and tensors, rather than a benchmark-driven evaluation.
infographic.watch_item: The guarantees depend on choosing a sampling notion that matches the domain’s symmetries, so a mismatch could weaken both approximation quality and generalization.
infographic.paper_title: Any-Dimensional Learning by Sampling

title: Higher-Order Geometric Updates for Levenberg-Marquardt Method via Riemann Normal Coordinates
abstract: Nonlinear least-squares optimization is central to regression, physics-informed neural networks, and other machine-learning tasks. Such problems have a natural geometric interpretation, model predictions form a manifold in data space, while the chosen parameterization can introduce parameter-effects curvature that becomes a dominant source of nonlinearity. This exposes a limitation of the Levenberg-Marquardt (LM) method, its tangent-space step is applied as a straight update in parameter coordinates. Geodesic acceleration gives a second-order correction, but its removal of parameter-effect curvature is exact only in the infinitesimal-step limit. We propose a Riemann-normal-coordinate Levenberg-Marquardt method (RNC-LM) to improve this consistency for finite optimization steps. By reformulating the geodesic equation, RNC-LM extends geodesic acceleration to arbitrary-order corrections and constructs finite-step updates with progressively higher reparameterization consistency. A line search along the resulting RNC curve controls the traveled distance while keeping the cost close to standard LM. The method eliminates the tangential component of residual acceleration order by order in a moving tangent frame, making the actual objective reduction more consistent with the linear model prediction of LM. On classical nonlinear least-squares benchmarks, RNC-LM improves convergence and robustness in curved valleys and rank-deficient problems. On a reaction-diffusion PINN failure-mode benchmark, it reduces the relative L2 error to the order of 1e-3 and recovers a physically meaningful solution. On a large-scale machine-learning potential-energy-surface fitting task, it achieves a 34-fold speedup over standard LM.

infographic.key_insight: RNC-LM improves Levenberg-Marquardt by using Riemann normal coordinates to add higher-order geometric corrections, making finite optimization steps more consistent with the local nonlinear manifold.
infographic.engineering_relevance: It can make nonlinear least-squares training more stable and accurate in curved, ill-conditioned problems such as PINNs, regression, and large-scale surrogate/model fitting.
method: Reformulates the geodesic equation in a moving tangent frame to compute higher-order update corrections.
Uses Riemann-normal-coordinate updates to reduce parameter-effects curvature beyond standard geodesic acceleration.
Adds a line search along the RNC curve to control step length while preserving low cost.
infographic.evidence_signal: The abstract reports improved convergence on classical benchmarks, reduced PINN relative L2 error to around 1e-3, and a 34-fold speedup on a large-scale potential-energy-surface fitting task.
infographic.watch_item: Performance may depend on accurate higher-order geometric terms and a well-tuned line search, which could add implementation complexity in large models.
infographic.paper_title: Higher-Order Geometric Updates for Levenberg-Marquardt Method via Riemann Normal Coordinates

title: Leveraging Extragradient for Effective Sharpness-Aware Minimization in Deep Learning
authors: Yao Fu, Chunxia Zhang, Junmin Liu, Yihang Jin, Haishan Ye, Yuanao Yang
abstract: Generalization remains a pivotal challenge in deep learning, where traditional optimizers like Stochastic Gradient Descent (SGD) often converge to sharp minima, leading to overfitting and reduced performance on unseen data. Building on Sharpness-Aware Minimization (SAM), for seeking flat minima associated with improved generalization, we propose the Extragradient-Inspired Sharpness-Aware Minimization (EISAM), a novel optimizer that enhances generalization via the extragradient technique. EISAM uses a two-step update process: a prediction step investigating the geometry of the loss landscape and a perturbation step that refines updates with a base optimizer. This approach achieves better generalization performance than SAM. Crucially, EISAM reduces sensitivity to the perturbation radius, enhancing robustness, and simplifying the tuning across diverse settings. Extensive experiments on benchmark datasets demonstrate that EISAM consistently outperforms SGD, Adaptive Moment Estimation (Adam), and SAM in test accuracy and training efficiency across various architectures. Theoretical analysis further confirms that EISAM tightens the generalization bound by steering parameters toward flatter minima with reduced curvature. Accompanied by a thorough hyperparameter analysis, EISAM offers practical tuning guidance, establishing it as a robust, scalable, and broadly applicable optimization solution that advances both the theory and practice in deep learning.

infographic.key_insight: EISAM improves deep-learning generalization by combining Sharpness-Aware Minimization with an extragradient-style two-step update that targets flatter minima more robustly than SAM.
infographic.engineering_relevance: It offers a practical optimizer for training deep models that is less sensitive to perturbation-radius tuning and can improve both test accuracy and training efficiency across architectures.
method: Prediction step explores the loss landscape geometry before updating.
Perturbation step refines the update using a base optimizer.
Designed to reduce curvature and steer parameters toward flatter minima.
infographic.evidence_signal: The abstract reports extensive benchmark experiments showing EISAM outperforms SGD, Adam, and SAM in test accuracy and training efficiency across multiple architectures.
infographic.watch_item: Performance still depends on hyperparameter choices, so the extra update step and perturbation settings may require careful implementation and tuning in new tasks.
infographic.paper_title: Leveraging Extragradient for Effective Sharpness-Aware Minimization in Deep Learning

title: Dynamics of Gradient Descent with Large Step Size Near a Manifold of Flat Minima
abstract: An important quantity in the theory of gradient descent (GD) is the \emph{sharpness}, defined as the largest eigenvalue of the objective Hessian. Classical analyses typically require the step size to be uniformly smaller than twice the reciprocal of the sharpness, but this condition is frequently violated in the training of deep neural networks. Recent work bridges this gap in the setting of overparametrised least-squares with a \emph{single scalar output}, providing a normal form for large-step GD in a neighbourhood of an \emph{isolated} flat minimum and establishing three corresponding convergence results. In this paper, we extend this theory in two directions: (1) to overparametrised least-squares with \emph{vector-valued outputs} (including regression with arbitrarily many observations), and (2) to a neighbourhood of a \emph{manifold} of flat minima (which we show is essential for applications such as matrix factorisation). We generalise both the normal form and all three convergence theorems of \cite{macdonaldeos} to this broader setting, overcoming several technical challenges, including the solution of a singular partial differential equation via a novel method that may be of independent interest. We further show that our framework applies to deep matrix factorisation under mild assumptions, yielding several new structural results. In particular, we prove that the set of flat minima forms a fibre bundle over a product of spheres, and that the sharpness is Morse-Bott along this manifold.

infographic.key_insight: Large-step gradient descent can still be characterized and shown to converge near flat minima even when those minima form a manifold and outputs are vector-valued.
infographic.engineering_relevance: This broadens the theoretical safety zone for using aggressive learning rates in overparameterized least-squares and matrix factorization models, including settings relevant to deep learning.
method: Generalize the large-step GD normal form from scalar-output problems to vector-valued outputs.
Extend the analysis from an isolated flat minimum to a manifold of flat minima.
Use a novel method to solve a singular PDE and derive convergence theorems plus structural results for deep matrix factorization.
infographic.evidence_signal: The abstract reports theoretical extensions and new structural theorems, but no experimental dataset or benchmark evaluation.
infographic.watch_item: The results rely on local neighborhood assumptions and mild structural conditions, so practical applicability may depend on verifying manifold geometry and sharpness behavior in a given model.
infographic.paper_title: Dynamics of Gradient Descent with Large Step Size Near a Manifold of Flat Minima

title: Reinforcing the Generation Order of Multimodal Masked Diffusion Models
authors: Yidong Ouyang, Zhe Wang, Sourav Bhabesh, Dmitriy Bespalov
abstract: Diffusion Language Models (DLMs) have recently achieved substantial progress in natural language generation tasks. Recent research demonstrates that adaptive token generation ordering can significantly improve performance in mathematical reasoning and code synthesis applications. In this work, we investigate the optimization of generation order for both text-to-image synthesis and multimodal understanding. We first establish that, unlike structured problems in language generation such as Sudoku puzzles, model logits alone are insufficient for determining optimal generation sequences in text-to-image generation and multimodal understanding. To address this challenge, we introduce a learnable control module trained via Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) to determine the generation order. Our results demonstrate that learning this control block substantially improves both text-to-image alignment and multimodal understanding in DLMs. In particular, it enhances the model's ability to capture fine-grained spatial relationships in generated images while also strengthening performance on multimodal reasoning and comprehension tasks. We evaluate our framework on GenEval, an object-focused benchmark for text-to-image alignment, where it achieves 4.08% relative improvements. In addition, experiments on VLMEvalKit confirm 4.85% relative improvements in multimodal understanding, highlighting the broad effectiveness of our approach.

infographic.key_insight: Adaptive generation order is not reliably inferred from logits alone for multimodal diffusion models, so a learned control module can improve how these models generate and understand content.
infographic.engineering_relevance: This suggests a practical way to boost text-to-image alignment and multimodal reasoning in diffusion-based systems by explicitly learning the token generation schedule.
method: Train a learnable control block to choose generation order
Optimize the control policy with Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO)
Apply the framework to both text-to-image synthesis and multimodal understanding
infographic.evidence_signal: The abstract reports 4.08% relative improvement on GenEval for text-to-image alignment and 4.85% relative improvement on VLMEvalKit for multimodal understanding.
infographic.watch_item: The added control module and RL training may increase system complexity and require careful tuning to generalize across tasks and modalities.
infographic.paper_title: Reinforcing the Generation Order of Multimodal Masked Diffusion Models

title: The Technological Turn in Mathematics
abstract: Quickly evolving technologies, such as Interactive Theorem Provers (ITPs), Automated Theorem Provers (ATPs), and Large Language Models (LLMs), all falling under the general heading 'AI for mathematics,' are transforming mathematical practice in profound ways. This chapter explores the implications of these innovations, focusing on their impact on how mathematical knowledge is created and shared. It also discusses how they are reshaping the social dimension of mathematics, altering collaboration dynamics, trust relationships, and the collective production of knowledge. For instance, tools like ITPs facilitate large-scale collaborations and make new types of teamwork possible, where trust is not a necessary ingredient. ITPs also help us mitigate our human fallibility, yet they raise questions about the nature of formalization and the relationship between traditional and formal mathematics. Technologies such as LLMs are reshaping the division of epistemic labour between humans and machines and urge philosophers of mathematics to ask questions about the value of their work.

infographic.key_insight: AI for mathematics—especially ITPs, ATPs, and LLMs—is changing not just how proofs are produced, but also how mathematical knowledge is formalized, trusted, and collaboratively created.
infographic.engineering_relevance: These tools suggest new human-AI workflows for verification, large-scale collaboration, and error reduction in formal reasoning systems.
method: Compare roles of ITPs, ATPs, and LLMs in mathematical practice
Analyze effects on collaboration, trust, and division of epistemic labor
Discuss implications for formalization versus traditional mathematics
infographic.evidence_signal: The chapter is conceptually grounded in philosophical analysis of technologies and their effects, rather than reporting a specific experimental dataset or benchmark evaluation.
infographic.watch_item: Formalization and LLM-assisted reasoning may shift responsibility and trust in ways that are powerful but difficult to validate consistently in practice.
infographic.paper_title: The Technological Turn in Mathematics

title: Learning Adaptive Coarse Spaces Using Transferable Neural Network Models for Linear and Nonlinear Overlapping Domain Decomposition Methods
authors: Axel Klawonn, Martin Lanser, Janine Weber-Hamacher
abstract: Domain decomposition methods have been established as efficient and parallel scalable iterative solvers and preconditioners for the solution of large-scale systems arising from the discretization of partial differential equations. In particular, overlapping Schwarz methods have been successfully applied to a wide range of linear and nonlinear problems. However, for problems with highly heterogeneous coefficients, standard domain decomposition methods typically suffer from deteriorating convergence rates. Robustness with respect to the coefficient contrast can be achieved by enriching the coarse space with adaptively selected constraints obtained from local generalized eigenvalue problems. The construction of these adaptive coarse spaces, however, can account for a significant part of the overall computing time. In the present work, machine learning techniques are employed to reduce this part of the computing time in the context of the adaptive Generalized Dryja-Smith-Widlund (AGDSW) coarse space. A two-stage approach is proposed in which regression neural networks are used to predict the adaptive coarse basis functions, while a classification neural network is employed to predict the number of basis functions required to ensure robustness. As a consequence, adaptive coarse spaces can be set up in the online phase without solving any eigenvalue problem. Particular attention is paid to problem-specific aspects, including sign-invariant loss functions and post-processing strategies to significantly improve the predicted constraints. The proposed approach is first investigated for scalar diffusion problems with high coefficient contrasts and is subsequently transferred, without retraining, to problems of linear elasticity and to nonlinear $p$-Laplace problems, also within a nonlinear Schwarz framework.

infographic.key_insight: The paper learns adaptive coarse spaces with neural networks so overlapping domain decomposition solvers can avoid costly local eigenvalue solves while preserving robustness for heterogeneous PDEs.
infographic.engineering_relevance: This can substantially reduce setup time in scalable multilevel solvers for large simulation workflows in diffusion, elasticity, and nonlinear PDEs.
method: Train regression networks to predict adaptive coarse basis functions.
Train a classification network to predict how many basis functions are needed for robustness.
Use sign-invariant losses and post-processing to improve constraint predictions; transfer the model across PDE types without retraining.
infographic.evidence_signal: The abstract reports testing on high-contrast scalar diffusion and then transferring the learned approach, without retraining, to linear elasticity and nonlinear p-Laplace problems in a nonlinear Schwarz framework.
infographic.watch_item: Performance depends on the quality of learned constraints, so incorrect basis-count or basis-shape predictions could weaken solver robustness in new problem settings.
infographic.paper_title: Learning Adaptive Coarse Spaces Using Transferable Neural Network Models for Linear and Nonlinear Overlapping Domain Decomposition Methods

title: TREK: Distill to Explore, Reinforce to Refine
authors: Yuanda Xu, Zhengze Zhou, Kayhan Behdin, Jelena Markovic-Voronov, Hejian Sang, Xiaomin Li
abstract: Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) is effective when the current policy already samples useful reasoning trajectories, but it stalls on hard prompts whose correct solution modes lie outside the student's on-policy support. We propose TREK (Teacher-Routed Exploration via Forward KL), a simple staged procedure that uses distillation not for imitation but for exploration support expansion. A key advantage of TREK is its generality: because it only consumes verified output trajectories, it can use an external black-box teacher, a white-box teacher, or the same model given additional inference-time context, and it can efficiently identify which hard-prompt samples are most worth consolidating even when teacher internals are unavailable. TREK first identifies prompts where the unaided student has very low pass rate, queries a proposal source to produce verified candidate solutions, keeps the top-$r$ proposals ranked by current student likelihood, applies a short forward-KL phase to pull those verified modes into the student's support, and then returns to standard on-policy GRPO refinement. On mathematical reasoning, TREK with DeepSeek-V4 proposals improves Qwen3 models across all tested scales on AIME 2024 and AIME 2025; for Qwen3-8B, it improves AIME 2025 from 36.9 to 40.3 and AIME 2024 from 47.9 to 51.1 (avg@16), while the self-context variant reaches 38.5 and 49.6 without an external teacher. On agentic tasks, TREK raises ALFWorld success rate from 75.8 to 82.8 and ScienceWorld success rate from 12.5 to 26.7; notably, on the hardest task types, TREK achieves high success rates early in training while unaided GRPO requires substantially more optimization steps to reach comparable levels.

infographic.key_insight: TREK expands a student model’s support on hard reasoning problems by using verified teacher-generated solutions for short forward-KL exploration, then switches back to GRPO for refinement.
infographic.engineering_relevance: It offers a practical training recipe for boosting hard-task reasoning and agent performance even when the teacher is a black box or only inference-time context is available.
method: Identify prompts where the student has very low pass rate.
Query a proposal source for verified candidate solutions and keep the top-r ranked by student likelihood.
Apply a short forward-KL distillation phase to pull those modes into support, then resume on-policy GRPO.
infographic.evidence_signal: On Qwen3-8B, TREK improves AIME 2025 from 36.9 to 40.3 and AIME 2024 from 47.9 to 51.1 (avg@16), and also boosts ALFWorld and ScienceWorld success rates.
infographic.watch_item: Performance depends on finding verified proposals and selecting useful hard prompts, so compute cost and proposal quality may limit gains in practice.
infographic.paper_title: TREK: Distill to Explore, Reinforce to Refine

title: MIRA-Math: A Benchmark for Minimal Information Requesting and Mathematical Reasoning
abstract: Mathematical reasoning benchmarks typically provide all facts needed to solve each problem, while interactive benchmarks often mix reasoning with tools, retrieval, and long-horizon dialogue. We introduce MIRA-Math, a benchmark for a narrower diagnostic capability: solving mathematical problems whose full latent state has a unique answer, but whose solver-facing view is missing exactly one necessary atomic fact. The solver must request the missing information in natural language under a strict budget and then integrate the returned fact into an exact final answer. A fixed constrained LLM responder sees only the dataset-provided atomic fact and must either offer the quoted fact when the request matches it, or decline otherwise. Thus, instance generation, typed hint specifications, validation, and final-answer verification are deterministic, while request metrics are measured under a fixed LLM-mediated responder channel. MIRA-Math contains 2{,}310 generated instances from 22 typed mathematical families spanning algebra, probability, linear systems, discrete structures, signal processing, Markov chains, circuits, interpolation, and numerical boundary-value problems. Experiments across frontier and small models show that request success and final-answer accuracy are separable: models may ask for the right fact yet fail the downstream computation, or fail before obtaining the canonical hint. We release generators, verifiers, prompts, run metadata, and dataset documentation to support reproducible evaluation of minimal information requesting in mathematical reasoning.

infographic.key_insight: MIRA-Math isolates a new skill in mathematical reasoning: correctly identifying and requesting exactly one missing atomic fact, then using it to reach an exact answer.
infographic.engineering_relevance: The benchmark provides a controlled, reproducible testbed for evaluating how well models can interactively acquire just enough information for computation under strict query constraints.
method: 2,310 generated problems across 22 mathematical families
Solver-facing view omits exactly one necessary atomic fact; the model must request it in natural language
A fixed LLM responder returns the quoted fact only when the request matches, enabling deterministic verification
infographic.evidence_signal: The abstract reports experiments showing request success and final-answer accuracy are separable, with evaluation performed across frontier and small models on a fixed responder-mediated channel.
infographic.watch_item: Because success depends on exact natural-language matching against a constrained responder, results may be sensitive to prompt phrasing and request formatting rather than reasoning alone.
infographic.paper_title: MIRA-Math: A Benchmark for Minimal Information Requesting and Mathematical Reasoning

title: Geometric--Nongeometric Optimizer Calculus: A Modular Language for Reachable Gradient Methods
abstract: Adaptive optimizers mix several mechanisms: a metric or preconditioner maps gradients to descent directions, while estimation, memory, step-size control, constraints, stochasticity, target modification, and discretization determine which directions are available and how they are used. We introduce geometric--nongeometric optimizer calculus, a modular language for auditing reachable gradient methods under explicit oracle, budget, state, and rule constraints. The geometric module is a positive cometric family that maps covectors to parameter-space directions; the nongeometric modules are information, memory, control, operator, noise, target, and discretization mechanisms. The main formal result is a direction-expressivity theorem: away from critical points, full positive-definite geometry expresses exactly the strict descent directions. We then define restricted direction residuals for admissible metric families, prove exact expressivity conditions for diagonal and block geometries, and separate this direction-level diagnostic from condition-number geometric complexity. The resulting design problem is a Pareto optimization over module budgets, not a single universal optimizer ordering. We also lift pointwise residuals to a trajectory-level residual complexity that couples direction mismatch with the variation of the explaining geometry. We include diagnostic prototypes only as evidence for the language: a high-information full-metric probe solves deterministic quadratic benchmarks to numerical precision, while a practical Muon-style PyTorch candidate gives small-scale evidence that matrix-operator updates can be audited by the calculus. The paper is a theory and benchmark-language manuscript; it does not claim large-scale optimizer state-of-the-art performance.

infographic.key_insight: The paper introduces a modular calculus that separates optimizer geometry from nongeometric mechanisms and proves that full positive-definite geometry exactly captures strict descent directions away from critical points.
infographic.engineering_relevance: It provides a design-audit framework for adaptive optimizers, helping engineers reason about which modules are needed to achieve specific update directions under budget, state, and oracle constraints.
method: Defines geometric modules as positive cometrics and nongeometric modules for information, memory, control, operators, noise, targets, and discretization.
Proves a direction-expressivity theorem plus exact residual conditions for diagonal and block metric families.
Lifts pointwise residuals to trajectory-level residual complexity that accounts for geometry variation over time.
infographic.evidence_signal: The abstract reports diagnostic prototypes only: a full-metric probe solves deterministic quadratic benchmarks to numerical precision, and a Muon-style PyTorch candidate offers small-scale auditing evidence.
infographic.watch_item: The framework is primarily theoretical and benchmark-language oriented, so its practical value depends on careful implementation and may not translate directly into large-scale optimizer performance gains.
infographic.paper_title: Geometric--Nongeometric Optimizer Calculus: A Modular Language for Reachable Gradient Methods

title: Restricted Dynamic Geometric Complexity: Certificates for Structured Preconditioning
abstract: Optimization geometrodynamics views optimizer state as evolving geometry. Its full positive-definite quadratic benchmark gives the least affine-invariant deformation needed to reduce condition number when arbitrary metrics are allowed. This paper records that benchmark in the present notation and develops restricted dynamic geometric complexity: an intrinsic certificate distance for reaching a target condition-number class when the metric is restricted to a specified family. The main proved results are monotonicity and submanifold-distance principles, diagonal and block reachability as linear matrix inequality feasibility problems, an exact two-dimensional diagonal complexity formula, and affine-invariant Kronecker projection theorems with normal equations, computable mismatch certificates, Armijo solver convergence, auxiliary self-conditioned K-target bounds, and Hessian-relative candidate certificates through an exact Kronecker Loewner-sandwich reachability condition, including a Kronecker expression threshold and a fixed-basis exact subproblem. Low-rank spectral models, curvature-proxy inflation, stochastic restricted complexity, discrete geometric length, and expression--estimation--flow--discretization accounting are presented as diagnostic interfaces rather than full optimizer characterizations. The resulting language turns structural preconditioner questions into geometric distance, reachability, and certificate problems. The repository includes deterministic toy and synthetic workflows that check diagonal expression gaps, block primal/dual certificates, Kronecker spectral width, and Hessian-relative Kronecker candidate certificates on small quadratic instances, together with low-rank spectral monotonicity.

infographic.key_insight: The paper reframes structured preconditioning as a geometric certificate problem: how far a restricted metric family is from achieving a target condition-number class.
infographic.engineering_relevance: This gives engineers a principled way to judge whether diagonal, block, Kronecker, or low-rank preconditioners are feasible before investing in solver tuning.
method: Defines restricted dynamic geometric complexity as an intrinsic distance-to-reachability measure under metric-family constraints.
Proves monotonicity and submanifold-distance principles, then casts diagonal/block reachability as LMI feasibility.
Derives exact or computable certificates for 2D diagonal cases, Kronecker projections, and Hessian-relative candidate tests.
infographic.evidence_signal: The abstract reports deterministic toy and synthetic workflows that verify diagonal, block, and Kronecker certificate behavior on small quadratic instances.
infographic.watch_item: Several results are diagnostic or certificate-based rather than full optimizer characterizations, so practical use depends on how well the chosen metric family matches the real problem structure.
infographic.paper_title: Restricted Dynamic Geometric Complexity: Certificates for Structured Preconditioning

title: Geometric Self-Distillation for Reasoning Generalization
abstract: On-policy distillation is a practical post-training recipe for large language models, supplying dense teacher supervision on the student's own trajectories. In privileged-context self-distillation, teacher and student are the same model conditioned on the same prefix, but the teacher also sees a hint or the full solution trace. This makes supervision abundant but harder to trust: the teacher can be confident about continuations its privileged view makes obvious but the student cannot yet justify. The distillation pull is strongest where teacher and student disagree most, and over many updates it accumulates into drift that degrades out-of-distribution (OOD) reasoning. We introduce GeoSD, a geometric self-distillation objective that treats this drift as movement in the student's predictive behavior and counters it in two complementary ways. A Hellinger loss scales each teacher preference by the overlap the student already shares with it, attenuating the pull on tokens the student cannot yet support. Since these pulls still compound over training, a proximal term penalizes how far the student's predictions drift from a recent checkpoint, measured as a Fisher-Rao distance. Both are distances in the same geometry of next-token distributions, and a natural-gradient update takes its steps in that geometry rather than in parameter space. Across mathematical reasoning benchmarks and three model families, GeoSD preserves the in-distribution gains of self-distillation while improving average OOD accuracy by 5.7-8.6 points over the base model, with gains holding across model scales from 1.7B to 32B. Analyzing why standard matching fails out of distribution, we find it wins agreement with the teacher by draining mass from alternatives at high-entropy states, resulting in confident agreement on wrong answers, whereas GeoSD keeps those alternatives in reach.

infographic.key_insight: GeoSD makes self-distillation safer for reasoning by scaling teacher guidance with student overlap and limiting prediction drift in the same probability geometry.
infographic.engineering_relevance: It offers a practical post-training recipe for LLMs that improves out-of-distribution mathematical reasoning without sacrificing in-distribution gains.
method: Hellinger loss weakens teacher pressure on tokens the student cannot currently support.
A proximal penalty uses Fisher-Rao distance to keep the student near a recent checkpoint.
Natural-gradient updates operate in distribution geometry rather than raw parameter space.
infographic.evidence_signal: Across mathematical reasoning benchmarks and three model families, GeoSD improves average OOD accuracy by 5.7–8.6 points over the base model at scales from 1.7B to 32B.
infographic.watch_item: The approach adds geometric regularization and checkpoint tracking, so implementation needs careful tuning to avoid under-updating or extra training complexity.
infographic.paper_title: Geometric Self-Distillation for Reasoning Generalization

title: Optimization Geometrodynamics: A Framework for Dynamic Geometric Optimization
abstract: Most gradient-based optimization methods move parameters through a fixed background geometry, even when their internal states implicitly define changing notions of length, curvature, and preconditioning. We introduce optimization geometrodynamics, a benchmark language in which optimization is a coupled evolution of a parameter trajectory, a transported distribution of particles, and a controlled time-varying Riemannian metric. The language separates invariant obstructions from improvable geometric mismatch: positive metrics preserve critical points and Morse indices, and cannot remove global geodesic-convexity obstructions, but can alter conditioning, distributional transport, and flux away from exact critical points. We introduce dynamic geometric complexity, the minimum geometric cost required to reduce an optimization difficulty observable. In the oracle benchmark model of strongly convex quadratic objectives with full positive-definite metric control, this complexity is exactly the affine-invariant distance from the relative log-spectrum to a low-condition-number set. We also analyze Hessian-matching flows, spectral Onsager relaxation, discrete exponential projection updates, gauge-invariant observables, and fixed-time local Morse-saddle flux. The paper is theory-only: its claims are formal statements with proofs, intended to provide invariants and benchmark costs against which implementable adaptive optimizers can be compared once their admissible metric families, curvature estimates, and discretization errors are specified.

infographic.key_insight: Optimization can be viewed as a coupled geometric system, where a time-varying metric changes conditioning and transport but cannot remove fundamental topological obstructions.
infographic.engineering_relevance: The framework gives optimizer designers a benchmark for measuring how much adaptive geometry can improve convergence, stability, and curvature handling under realistic metric constraints.
method: Define optimization geometrodynamics: a parameter path, transported particle distribution, and controlled Riemannian metric evolve together.
Separate invariant obstacles from improvable geometric mismatch using positive-metric invariants, gauge-invariant observables, and flux/curvature analyses.
Derive dynamic geometric complexity and compute it exactly for strongly convex quadratics with full metric control via affine-invariant spectral distance.
infographic.evidence_signal: The paper is theory-only and supports its claims with formal statements and proofs, including an exact benchmark result for the oracle quadratic setting.
infographic.watch_item: The framework is only directly actionable once the optimizer’s admissible metric family, curvature estimates, and discretization errors are specified.
infographic.paper_title: Optimization Geometrodynamics: A Framework for Dynamic Geometric Optimization
